rulings.Case Title: CONTEST PROMOTIONS, LLC, VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO
rulings.Calendar Matter: Respondent City And County Of San Francisco Motion For Summary Judgment , And In The Alternative For Summary Adjudication (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 2 *** Plaintiff's regulatory contract cases are distinguishable. In Pure Wafer Inc. v. City of Prescott (9th Cir. 2017) 845 F.3d 943, the City of Prescott made a specific promise to Pure Wafer that it would accept its effluent so long as its non-pretreated fluoride content remained at or below 100mg/L. Prescott made that promise as a way to entice Pure Wafer to build its facility there. Similarly, in U.S. v. Winstar Corp. (1996) 518 U.S. 839, the government made a particular promise to lock in the then-current regulatory treatment of supervisory goodwill to induce the plaintiff to take over an ailing thrift. There is no such express promise in the settlement agreement in this case. "When a contract is reduced to writing, the intention of the parties is to be ascertained from the writing alone, if possible . . . ." (Civ. Code § 1639.) While it is unnecessary to go beyond the plain language of the settlement agreement, the Court's conclusion is bolstered by the extrinsic evidence of the parties' settlement negotiations, during which Plaintiff unsuccessfully sought contract language that would have protected its signs against future regulatory changes, and by the fact that proposed legislation amending the ordinance was pending throughout the period that the parties were negotiating the settlement agreement. (See Avidity Partners, LLC, 221 Cal.App.4th at 1184 ["The term Avidity seeks to imply . . . was specifically rejected in negotiations."]; Morey v. Vannucci (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 904, 912 ["The mutual intention to which the courts give effect is determined by objective manifestations of the parties' intent, including the words used in the agreement, as well as extrinsic evidence of such objective matters as the surrounding circumstances under which the parties negotiated or entered into the contract; the object, nature and subject matter of the contract; and the subsequent conduct of the parties."].) Treating plaintiff's signs as "business signs" was not essential to the settlement agreement and failing to do so does not render the agreement illusory. A major component of the settlement was to resolve the federal litigation and notices of violation ("NOVs"). The City sought almost a million dollars for the NOVs but agreed to accept $375,000. The City therefore gave ample consideration notwithstanding the business sign issue. Plaintiff cannot establish the breach of implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing cause of action. The implied covenant rests upon the existence of a specific contractual obligation and in this case, there was none. (See Avidity Partners, LLC, 221 Cal.App.4th at 1203-08.) To the extent plaintiff argues that the City breached the implied covenant by failing to treat plaintiff's signs as business signs, the claim is superfluous. (Id. at 1203.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** End of Tentative Ruling *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CONTEST PROMOTIONS, LLC, VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , And In The Alternative For Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, March 26, 2019, line 1. RESPONDENT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, And In The Alternative For Summary Adjudication. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 2 *** Respondent, Defendant, and Cross-Complainant City and County of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment is granted. The City is entitled to summary judgment on plaintiff's Fourth Amended Complaint and the City's Cross-Complaint. The July 16, 2014 settlement agreement between the parties is not a "regulatory contract," as the City never made a specific promise to accord plaintiff any sort of particular regulatory treatment. Plaintiff's argument that the City unconditionally promised to treat its signs as "business signs" fails. The settlement provides that plaintiff's signs "shall be deemed Business Signs . . . so long as they are consistent with . . . the Planning Code." (Settlement Agreement, 1 [emphasis added].) Other provisions of the settlement agreement show that the City never made an unconditional promise to insure regulatory stability. Plaintiff was obligated within 270 days of the effective date of the settlement agreement to apply for permits for its existing signs, and knew that those signs would need to comply with the law "in effect at the time the permit of the subject sign is issued." (Settlement Agreement, 2(a), 2(c) and 3.) The quoted language from 3 makes it clear that plaintiff's obligations may change based on successor rules. Indeed, Plaintiff itself concedes that "the City did not make an unconditional promise to issue permits for Contest's signs." (Memo. at 19.) Plaintiff concedes that its applications did not comply with the amended version of Planning Code § 602.3. Defendant did not breach the settlement agreement. (Avidity Partners, LLC v. State of California (2013) 221 Cal.App.4th 1180, 1194-1202 [no breach where there was no express provision assuring the State would allow Pacific Lumber to harvest a set amount of timber]; see also Admiral Financial Corp. v. U.S. (Fed. Cir. 2004) 378 F.3d 1336, 1339-40 [by terms of its contract with Federal Home Loan Bank Board, company that acquired savings and loan assumed the risk of future regulatory changes, and enactment of new federal statute did not breach government's contractual obligations]; County of Humboldt v. McKee (2008) 165 Cal.App.4th 1476, 1491-1500 [county did not breach land conservation contract with landowner by revising agricultural preserve guidelines to increase minimal parcel size for divisions of land, where guidelines were "properly interpreted to require compliance with the local and state laws in existence at the time of the land divisions."]; see generally Doe v. Harris (2013) 57 Cal.4th 64, 70 ["'When persons enter into a contract or transaction creating a relationship infused with a substantial public interest, subject to plenary control by the state, such contract or transaction is deemed to incorporate and contemplate not only the existing law but the reserve power of the state to amend the law or enact additional laws for the public good and in pursuance of public policy.'"].) *** End of Part 1 - Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2 *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GEORGE J. PETTY VS. SAN FRANCISCO EMPLOYEES' RETIREMENT SYSTEM
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, March 26, 2019, line 2. PETITIONER GEORGE PETTY'S Hearing For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 2 *** Petitioner George Petty's petition for writ of administrative mandamus is granted and the administrative decision denying Mr. Petty's application for industrial disability is set aside. Based on its independent review of the administrative record and weighing of the evidence, while affording a strong presumption of correctness to the administrative findings, the Court finds that the administrative determination as to disability and industrial causation is not supported by the weight of the evidence. (See Usher v. County of Monterey (1998) 65 Cal.App.4th 210, 219-20.) The hearing officer relied on Dr. Missirian's conclusions to find that Mr. Petty was not permanently disabled; however, Dr. Missirian's report does not constitute substantial evidence supporting such a finding. Dr. Missirian's opinion that Mr. Petty could return to the duties he was performing before Mr. Petty "decided" to retire misstates the facts and does not support the hearing officer's conclusion that Mr. Petty was not incapacitated. Mr. Petty could not have continued to perform his modified duties. His request to do so was denied because such accommodation was indisputably unavailable. Moreover, a finding that Mr. Petty could have performed modified duties does not support a conclusion that Mr. Petty was not substantially unable to perform his usual duties per Harmon v. Board of Retirement (1976) 62 Cal.App.3d 689, 695. (AR40.) The evidence shows that following the March 2013 incident, Mr. Petty was never able to perform the regular duties of a firefighter and was continually subjected to work restrictions incompatible with such duties. *** Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2 *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GEORGE J. PETTY VS. SAN FRANCISCO EMPLOYEES' RETIREMENT SYSTEM
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner George Petty Hearing For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: *** TENTATIVE RULING - PART 2 OF 2 *** The hearing officer's finding that any incapacity was a result of degenerative changes due to age and not to industrial causation is not supported by the weight of the evidence. In his findings of fact, the hearing officer found incorrectly that neither Dr. Missirian nor Dr. Purchase concluded that Mr. Petty's March 2013 fall precluded him from working full duty. (AR 9, 12.) However, Dr. Purchase's report expressly stated his opinion, as required by law, as to the apportionment of the permanent disability based on causation and found that 75% of the cause for the permanent disability was a direct result of injury arising out of employment. (AR 47.) Dr. Purchase imposed work restrictions that were incompatible with the regular duties of a firefighter. There is no evidence Mr. Petty had any medical problems with his knee prior to the work-related incident. The initial evaluation on March 9, 2013 found signs of trauma and swelling. (AR 183.) On March 11, 2013, the treating physician diagnosed Mr. Petty with a sprained knee, found that examination was consistent with Mr. Petty's report of the injury, and immediately placed him on modified duty (AR176). Further evaluations and imaging showed findings of cartilage thinning, edema and no fracture and Mr. Petty was diagnosed with chondromalacia patella consistent with his account of the onset of injury. (AR 121.) Substantial evidence supports a finding that the work-related injury contributed substantially to Mr. Petty's permanent disability. (Valero v. Board of Retirement of Tulare County Employees' Retirement Assn. (2012) 205 Cal.App.4th 960, 964.) The hearing officer relied on Dr. Missirian's report, but even that report did not conclude that Mr. Petty's knee issues were due solely to age. (AR 41). Although, under Nash v. Workers' Comp. Appeals Bd. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 1793, 1810, a single medical report, even when in conflict with other medical opinion, can be substantial evidence supporting the administrative determination as to industrial causation, Dr. Missirian's report admittedly relied upon an incomplete examination of medical records, while speculating that a complete review would not change his opinion. (AR 37.) Medical opinions would not be substantial evidence "if they are known to be erroneous, or if they are based on facts no longer germane, on inadequate medical histories and examinations, or on incorrect legal theories. . . if [they are] based on surmise, speculation, conjecture, or guess." (Hegglin v. Workmen's Comp. App. Bd. (1971) 4 Cal.3d 162, 169-70.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for petitioner is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** END OF TENTATIVE RULING *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GUILLAUME GASSUAN ET AL VS. BENEVOLENT AND PROTECTIVE ORDER OF ELKS OF THE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Award Of Reasonable Attorney Fees
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, March 26, 2019, line 3. PETITIONERS GUILLAUME GASSUAN, TODD MORENO'S Motion For Award Of Reasonable Attorney Fees. Petitioners Guillaume Gassuan and Todd M. Moreno's motion for an award of reasonable attorney fees is granted in the total amount of $16,613.70. The award is made pursuant to Civil Code section 1717 and Section 9.400 of the Laws of the Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks of the United States. By amendment dated July 3, 2018, Respondent BPOE amended Section 9.400 to provide in pertinent part as follows: "In any action brought by the Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks of the United States of America against any Member, Lodge or State Association, such Member, Lodge or State Association . . . further agrees that in any such action, upon entry of judgment in favor of the Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks of the United States, the offending Member, Lodge or State Association shall be responsible for reimbursement of all costs and reasonable attorney's fees incurred." The parties agree that this provision constitutes an enforceable contract between the BPOE and its members. (See, e.g., Thatcher v. City Terrace Cultural Center (1960) 181 Cal.App.2d 433, 443.) Respondent BPOE does not dispute that although the amendment on its face is a unilateral provision that permits only the BPOE to recover its fees if it prevails in an action brought against a member, Section 1717 renders the provision mutual. (e.g., Scott Co. of California v. Blount, Inc. (1999) 20 Cal.4th 1103, 1106 [section 1717 "provides that any contractual attorney fees provision must be applied mutually and equally to all parties to the contract, even if it is written otherwise"].) Nor does Respondent dispute that Petitioners prevailed in this action by virtue of the Court's January 30, 2019 order. Respondents contend that because the amendment of Section 9.400 did not become effective until August 3, 2018, it was not part of the contract between Petitioners and the BPOE at the time Petitioners' claims arose, and Petitioners therefore are not entitled to seek attorney fees under that provision. Not so. A new statute authorizing an award of attorney fees applies to actions not yet final at the time the statute becomes effective. (California Housing Finance Agency v. E.R. Fairway Associates I (1995) 37 Cal.App.4th 1508, 1513.) The same principle logically applies here, since the new bylaw became effective before the Court issued its order in Petitioners' favor, and there is no language in Section 9.400 that suggests any intent to limit its application to actions filed after its effective date. Respondents also challenge the amount of fees and costs sought by Petitioners as unreasonable. The Court disagrees, and finds both the amount of time for which compensation is sought and the hourly fees requested eminently reasonable. Respondents' objection to certain costs sought by Petitioners, including costs of photocopies and transcripts, lacks merit. Section 9.400 entitles the prevailing party to recover "all costs" incurred, not just those authorized by Code of Civil Procedure section 1033.5. (See Arntz Contracting v. St. Paul Fire & Marine (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 464, 492.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for petitioners is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

DEFENDANTS GUERNEVILLE BUSINESS CORPORATION, INC, RAY SHAHANI'S Motion For Leave To Amend Answer To Second Amended Complaint. Defendants Guerneville Business Corporation and Ray K. Shahani's motion for leave to amend answer to second amended complaint is granted on the following conditions: (1) within 5 days of the Court's order, Defendants shall serve a verified discovery response identifying each of the attorneys other than Alexander James Perez on whose advice Defendants claim to have relied; (2) Mr. Shahani shall make himself available for deposition prior to the April 12 discovery cutoff date on the new affirmative defense; and (3) the February 8, 2019 Order Granting Motion to Re-Open Discovery is modified to allow the parties to serve subpoenas on Mr. Perez and any other third-party attorneys as necessary to require their depositions. California's policy of liberality in pleading warrants granting the motion. Plaintiff and Cross-Defendant Arthur O. Fonden and Cross-Defendant Scott J. Mandell have not shown they would be prejudiced by allowing the amendment. rulings.Case Title: ANTON ANDRYEYEV VS. AZIZEH BAJJALIYA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Azizeh Bajjaliya Motion To Compel Compliance For Production Of Documents, Set Two
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, March 26 2019, Line 5, Defendant Azizeh Bajjaliya's Motion To Compel Compliance For Production Of Documents, Set Two. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The parties shall address production of documents responsive to RFP categories 23, 24, 25, 26, 27, 28, 29, 31, 32, 33, 34. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ** TRANSFERRED TO ORANGE COUNTY ** ROBERT ROSARIO VS. DIGNITY HEALTH ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Change Venue Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure Of Defendants, Dignity, U.S. Healthworks Medical Group, Sheila Nothcutt, Pt And Lorraine Sunday, M.D.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, March 26, 2019, line 7. DEFENDANTS SHEILA NORTHCUTT, LORRAINE SUNDAY, DIGNITY HEALTH, U.S. HEALTHWORKS MEDICAL GROUP'S Motion To Change Venue Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure. Defendants Dignity Health, U.S. HealthWorks Medical Group, Sheila Northcutt, P.T., and Lorraine Sunday, M.D.'s motion to change venue pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 397 is granted. While this Court's February 20, 2018 Order denied a previous motion to transfer venue under Code of Civil Procedure Section 395 because venue is proper in San Francisco County, that order specifically preserved the right of Defendants to seek a motion to transfer venue for convenience of the witnesses under Code of Civil Procedure section 397(c). The convenience of witnesses and the interests of justice support transfer of this action to Orange County. It is undisputed that Defendants Northcutt, Sunday, and Byron Ted Field, M.D. all reside in Orange County and work at a U.S. HealthWorks facility in Orange County. The incident in which Plaintiff was injured occurred at Plaintiff Robert Rosario's place of employment in Anaheim, which is in Orange County. The alleged medical negligence occurred at a facility in Orange County. As supported by Plaintiff's responses to Dr. Field's Interrogatory 6.4, all of Plaintiff's medical care in the wake of the accident occurred in Southern California-in Orange County, Los Angeles County, Riverside County, and San Diego County. (Quilligan Decl. Exh. B, p. 11.) Plaintiff further responded to Interrogatory 2.5 that his residence is in nearby Riverside County. (Id. at p. 4.) Plaintiff's opposition to the motion offers no contrary evidence, and verges on the frivolous. As in Richfield Hotel Management, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 22 Cal.App.4th 222, 227, all of the relevant events took place in another county, all of the witnesses live and work in that county, all of the relevant documents and records are located in that county, and Plaintiff has offered no evidence to suggest that any witnesses would be inconvenienced. It would therefore be an abuse of discretion for the Court to deny this motion where the witnesses would clearly be burdened by the need to travel to San Francisco. (Id.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: EDUARDO MONTEAGUDO ET AL VS. GREENTREE PROPERTY MANAGEMENT ET AL

Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. rulings.Case Title: STEVEN MOORE VS. ZIRX TRANSPORTATION SERVICES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Approve Paga Settlement And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, March 26, 2019, line 9. PLAINTIFF STEVEN MOORE'S Motion To Approve PAGA Settlement. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 3 *** Plaintiff Steven Moore's motion to approve PAGA settlement is denied without prejudice. As a threshold matter, Plaintiff incorrectly contends that PAGA requires judicial approval only of the penalties that are sought as part of a proposed settlement, but "does not require that a court approve any other elements of the proposed settlement, including the Plaintiff's individual settlement and the attorneys' fees and costs." (Mot. at 8.) Plaintiff mistakenly relies upon a prior version of Labor Code section 2699(l) that was amended in 2016. Labor Code section 2699(l)(2) currently provides, "The superior court shall review and approve any settlement of any civil action filed pursuant to this part." That requirement extends to all elements of the proposed settlement, not only the amount of civil penalties. (See generally Williams v. Superior Court (2017) 3 Cal.5th 531, 549 ["PAGA settlements are subject to trial court review and approval, ensuring that any negotiated resolution is fair to those affected. (Lab. Code, § 26990, subd. (l)(2)."].) While the standard for approval of a PAGA settlement is still evolving, at a minimum the Court must determine whether the proposed settlement is fair and adequate in view of the purposes and policies of the statute, taking into account such factors as the adequacy of the civil penalties in light of the potential maximum if the matter were to go to verdict; the risks of litigation; the allocation of the total settlement consideration as between PAGA and other claims, such as individual or class claims; and the inclusion of any substantial injunctive or other non-monetary relief. (See O'Connor v. Uber Technologies, Inc. (N.D. Cal. 2016) 201 F.Supp.3d 1110, 1132-35.) In addition, a request for an award of attorneys' fees and costs to counsel requires judicial approval, both because PAGA's fee-shifting provision entitles a prevailing employee only to "an award of reasonable attorney's fees and costs," and because such an award reduces the net compensation to be paid to aggrieved employees, which necessarily affects the overall fairness of the settlement. Here, the moving papers fail to establish that the proposed settlement agreement should be approved in its current form, for the following reasons. *** End of Part 1 - Tentative Continues in Part 2 of 3 *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STEVEN MOORE VS. ZIRX TRANSPORTATION SERVICES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Steven Moore'S Motion To Approve Paga Settlement (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 3 *** First, Plaintiff does not provide sufficient information for the Court to evaluate the fairness and adequacy of the allocation of the settlement consideration. Counsel's declaration does address the maximum PAGA penalties Plaintiff's expert determined would be recoverable and the bases for that calculation, thus allowing a determination that the proposed $280,000 would represent approximately 36.3 percent of that amount, and discusses the grounds for discounting the maximum recovery in settlement, which appear reasonable. However, it does not explain the basis for allocating $40,000 of the PAGA Payment to civil penalties and the remainder of that payment after payment of attorneys' fees and expenses and administrative costs to underpaid wages payable directly to employees under Labor Code section 558. Such payments are an exception to the general rule that civil penalties in a PAGA action are distributed 75 percent to the Labor and Workforce Development Agency and 25 percent to the aggrieved employees. (Lab. Code § 2699(i); see Thurman v. Bayshore Transmit Management, Inc. (2012) 203 Cal.App.4th 1112, 1145.) Based on the calculations provided in counsel's declaration, civil penalties attributable to the section 558 claims represented approximately 27.5 percent of the maximum recoverable civil penalties. In contrast, the net payment to be allocated entirely to the Representative Group under section 558, after payment of $30,000 in civil penalties to the LWDA, $10,000 in civil penalties to the Representative Group, the proposed Individual Settlement Payment to Plaintiff ($10,000), proposed Attorneys' Fees ($93,333.33) and Expenses ($9,806.39) and Administration Costs ($4,500), represents approximately 43.7 percent of the PAGA Payment [$122,360.28/$280,000]. No explanation is provided for the apparent discrepancy. Second, no justification has been provided for the proposed "Individual Settlement Payment" to Plaintiff of $10,000, which is in addition to the Individual PAGA Payment to be paid to Plaintiff. The Court interprets this payment as an incentive payment, but has not been provided with any information regarding the basis for the payment or Plaintiff's involvement in the action. Third, Paragraph 37 of the Settlement Agreement provides that the Representative Group releases the Released Parties from the Released Claims, and the Representative Group shall be barred under the doctrine of res judicata from prosecuting any Released Claims. Similarly, Paragraph 42 of the Settlement Agreement provides that the court approval order and Judgment will contain provisions enjoining the Representative Group from prosecuting the Released Claims. The Court will not approve these provisions. The Representative Group members received no notice of this action or the settlement or the hearing on this motion, and cannot be bound by such a release. Further, injunctive relief is not available in a PAGA action. *** End of Part 2 - Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 3 of 3 *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STEVEN MOORE VS. ZIRX TRANSPORTATION SERVICES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Steven Moore'S Motion To Approve Paga Settlement (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: *** Tentative Ruling - Part 3 of 3 *** Fourth, Plaintiff has not shown that the request for an award of one-third of the gross settlement compensation ($93,333.33) as attorneys' fees is reasonable. Although the standard for an award of attorneys' fees under PAGA has not been clearly established, the usual method for calculating an attorneys' fees award under a statutory fee-shifting provision is the lodestar approach. (Laffitte v. Robert Half Int'l Inc. (2016) 1 Cal.5th 480, 500.) In the analogous context of class action litigation, while an attorneys' fees award is not per se unreasonable merely because it is calculated as a percentage of a common fund preserved or recovered by means of litigation, "[t]he choice of a fee calculation method is generally one within the discretion of the trial court, the goal under either the percentage or lodestar approach being the award of a reasonable fee to compensate counsel for their efforts." (Id. at 504.) Further, even if a court selects the percentage fee, it generally should double-check the reasonableness of the percentage fee through a lodestar calculation, which "provides a mechanism for bringing an objective measure of the work performed into the calculation of a reasonable attorney fee." (Id. at 488, 504.) Here, the Court intends to utilize both methods. Because Plaintiff has provided only very limited information regarding the tasks performed by counsel during the litigation, and no information whatever regarding which individual attorneys worked on the case, the number of hours worked, the hourly rates sought for that work, or the reasonableness of those rates, the Court cannot determine whether the fee award sought is reasonable and will not approve the requested award. Fifth, Plaintiff has not provided any information regarding the qualifications or experience of the designated Settlement Administrator, ILYM Group, Inc., or documenting its agreement to administer this settlement. Sixth, the settlement agreement contains apparently inconsistent provisions regarding the resolution of any future disputes. Paragraph 56 provides that following approval of the settlement, the Court will have continuing jurisdiction pursuant to California Code of Civil Procedure section 664.6 for purposes of addressing, among other things, the interpretation and enforcement of the terms of the Settlement. Paragraph 68 states that any dispute between the parties as to the remaining terms of the Settlement Agreement shall be presented to the mediator, Hon. Irma Gonzalez (Ret.) for resolution. If the parties desire the Court to retain jurisdiction over them to enforce the settlement until performance in full of its terms, the agreement should expressly so state, consistent with Code of Civil Procedure section 664.6. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** End of Tentative Ruling *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: EXPONENT, INC. VS. MECO CONSTRUCTORS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Plaintiffs Service Of Summons And To Dismiss Action For Lack Of In Personam Jurisdiction, Or, In The Alternative, To Dismiss Or Stay On Grounds Of Forum Non Conveniens Mpa
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, March 26, 2019, line 13. DEFENDANT MECO CONSTRUCTORS, INC'S Motion To Quash Plaintiff's Service Of Summons And To Dismiss Action For Lack Of In Personam Jurisdiction, Or, In The Alternative, To Dismiss Or Stay On Grounds Of Forum Non Conveniens. Defendant Meco Constructors, Inc.'s motion to quash is denied. Meco is subject to the California forum selection clause in paragraph 13 of the "TERMS AND CONDITIONS OF AGREEMENT." The Court overrules Meco's objections to the Whitehouse declaration. "He who takes the benefit must bear the burden" (Civ. Code § 3521.) "A voluntary acceptance of the benefit of a transaction is equivalent to a consent to all the obligations arising from it, so far as the facts are known, or ought to be known, to the person accepting." (Id. § 1589.) "It is clear that a party will not be permitted to receive and enjoy benefits that he knows are being offered to him at a price without paying for them, if he had an opportunity to reject them when they were offered without any expense or material inconvenience. Silence may here consummate his acceptance because the facts fairly call upon him to speak." (Durgin v. Kaplan (1968) 68 Cal.2d 81, 91.) In this case, it is undisputed that plaintiff's consulting services were for Meco's benefit and Meco never objected to the provision of those services. Meco fails to meet its "heavy burden" of proving that enforcement of the forum selection clause would be unreasonable under the circumstances of the case. (See Bancomer, S.A. v. Superior Court (1996) 44 Cal.App.4th 1450, 1457.) Meco's motion to stay or dismiss based on forum non conveniens is denied. Plaintiff's principal place of business is located in California. (Whitehouse Dec., 3.) The parties agreed to California jurisdiction and as a California resident, there is a strong presumption in favor of plaintiff's choice of forum. (See Ford Motor Co. v. Insurance Co. of North America (1995) 35 Cal.App.4th 604, 611; National Football League v. Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 902, 926-27.) Meco fails to show that the balance of public and private factors weighs in favor of granting its motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
